w: Founded between 1509 and 1513 by Zen priest Kogaku Soko, this sub-temple of Daitoku-ji holds one of Japan's finest dry landscape gardens. The screen paintings and the garden itself are attributed to Soami (d. 1525), whose diluted-ink mists channel Song dynasty China into a Kyoto courtyard. The name means 'Academy of the Great Immortals.' One of Rinzai Zen's five great temples — yet pocket-sized and almost silent.
_facts: Founded 1509-1513 by Zen priest Kogaku Soko
One of five most important Zen temples of Kyoto
Screen paintings and garden attributed to Soami (d. 1525), Zen monk and ink painter
Soami's work described by art historian Miyeko Murase as 'the very essence of the serenity of nature'
Name means 'Academy of the Great Immortals'

s: A plague shrine that became Kyoto's health pilgrimage
w: Originally built to appease the spirits causing epidemics, Imamiya-jinja sits in a forest of giant trees northwest of Daitoku-ji, the word 'imamiya' meaning 'newly constructed.' Every second Sunday in April it hosts the Yasurai Matsuri, one of the three great festivals of Kyoto, where demon-costumed dancers scatter cherry blossoms to pacify restless spirits. Outside the gates, two rival aburi-mochi stalls have faced each other across the lane for over a thousand years.
_facts: Originally established to pray for safety from epidemic
Yasurai Matsuri held every second Sunday of April, one of 3 major festivals in Kyoto
Name 'imamiya' means 'newly constructed'
Forest of large trees northwest of Daitoku-ji
Two aburi-mochi (grilled rice cake) shops outside, Ichiwa claims founding in 1000 CE

s: The mingei potter who refused Japan's highest honour
w: Kawai Kanjiro (1890-1966) built his own noborigama climbing kiln in Kyoto in 1920 after growing disillusioned with academic ceramics, and went on to become a cornerstone of the mingei folk-art movement alongside Bernard Leach and Hamada Shoji. He and Hamada are said to have conducted over 10,000 glaze experiments together. When offered the title of Living National Treasure, he declined: 'My work itself is my best signature.' His restored house in Gojozaka, warm with tansu and tatami, is run by his family today.
_facts: Kawai Kanjiro (1890-1966), key figure in mingei (Japanese folk art) movement
Built his own climbing kiln in Kyoto in 1920
Conducted over 10,000 glaze experiments with Hamada Shoji
Declined the designation of Living National Treasure
Said 'My work itself is my best signature' — never signed his pots
House restored in 1973, now a museum run by his relatives

s: Where Yoshida Shinto was born — and its founder buried
w: Founded in 859 by the Fujiwara clan on a wooded hill beside Kyoto University, Yoshida-jinja became one of the Twenty-Two Shrines receiving Imperial messengers from Emperor Murakami in 965. It is most famous as the seat of Yoshida Kanetomo (1435-1511), founder of Yoshida Shinto, the influential school that sought to place Shinto above Buddhism and Confucianism — and who is buried in the shrine grounds. Each February 2-3, a great bonfire here marks the Setsubun ritual for all of Japan.
_facts: Founded 859 by the Fujiwara clan
Added to Emperor Murakami's list of shrines receiving Imperial messengers in 991
Yoshida Kanetomo, founder of Yoshida Shinto, is buried here
One of the Twenty-Two Shrines
Famous for Setsubun bonfire ceremony in February

s: A Catholic daimyo's cross hidden in a Zen rock garden
w: This sub-temple of Daitoku-ji was founded by Otomo Sorin (1530-1587), the most powerful Catholic daimyo in Japan, who converted to Christianity after meeting Jesuit missionaries. Within the temple's classic karesansui garden, certain rocks are said to be arranged to form a hidden Christian cross — a quiet defiance encoded in raked gravel. Sorin sent the first Japanese embassy to the Pope in 1582. The garden was built during his lifetime, its secret still debated.
_facts: Sub-temple of Daitoku-ji, founded by daimyo Otomo Sorin (1530-1587)
Otomo Sorin was one of the few daimyo to convert to Catholicism
Rocks in the garden are said to form a hidden Christian cross
Sorin sent the Tensho Embassy to Rome in 1582, the first Japanese ambassadors to the Pope
Garden designated a Notable Place of Scenic Beauty

s: A cherry festival running since 985 CE, Kyoto's oldest
w: Emperor Kammu founded Hirano-jinja in 794, the year he moved the capital to Heian-kyo, enshrining four deities including the 'newly arrived' Imaki-no-Sume-Ookami. The shrine crest is the yaezakura, the double cherry blossom, and since 985 CE a festival has been held here every spring — making it the oldest continuously held festival in Kyoto. Over 400 cherry trees of 60 varieties bloom here, including rare grafted cultivars found nowhere else. Typhoon Jebi destroyed the worship hall in 2018; it is slowly being restored.
_facts: Established 794 CE by Emperor Kammu when capital moved to Heian-kyo
Cherry blossom festival held here since 985 CE — oldest regularly held festival in Kyoto
Festival began during reign of Emperor Kazan
Over 400 cherry trees, some rare cultivars unique to the shrine
Shrine crest is the yaezakura (double cherry blossom)

s: Oldest Zen temple in Kyoto — and where tea came to Japan
w: Founded in 1202 by monk Myoan Eisai with the support of Minamoto no Yoriie, Kennin-ji is Kyoto's oldest Zen temple and head of the Kennin-ji branch of Rinzai. Eisai, who introduced both Zen Buddhism and tea-drinking to Japan from China, is buried in the grounds. Dogen, later founder of the Soto school, trained here. The ceiling of the Dharma Hall carries a colossal twin-dragon painting by Koizumi Junsaku, installed in 2002 to mark the temple's 800th anniversary — painted first in a school gymnasium.
_facts: Founded 1202 by Myoan Eisai — oldest Zen temple in Kyoto
Eisai credited with introducing Zen Buddhism and tea-drinking to Japan; buried here
Dogen, founder of Soto Zen, trained at Kennin-ji
Twin-dragon ceiling painting by Koizumi Junsaku (1924-2012), installed 2002 for 800th anniversary
Head temple of the Kennin-ji branch of Rinzai Buddhism, c.72 affiliated temples

s: Three centuries of whisked tea on Teramachi
w: Founded in 1717 in Kyoto's Kyoho era, Ippodo has been blending and selling gyokuro, matcha and sencha from the same Teramachi address for three hundred years. The shop is a shinise — a family business passed down through generations — and occupies a corner of the district where Uji merchants once ran Japan's tea trade. Its attached tearoom, Kaboku, lets you try a bowl of thick koicha or a pot of gyokuro brewed by staff who know more about leaves than most people know about anything. No theatre. Just tea.
_facts: Founded 1717 (Kyoho 2nd year) in Kyoto
One of Japan's most prestigious tea specialist shops
Specialises in matcha, gyokuro and sencha from Uji and other growing regions
Official meta description on ippodo-tea.co.jp confirms 1717 founding date
Part of the shinise tradition of multigenerational family businesses

s: The tiny geiko shrine above Gion's willow canal
w: A vermilion torii barely taller than a person marks this miniature shrine at the south end of the Shirakawa Canal, where weeping willows trail over the water and the rear facades of Gion's ochaya teahouses line the bank. Geiko and maiko from the Gion hanamachi district come here to pray for success in their arts — dance, shamisen, the art of conversation. The stone lanterns are worn smooth. In April a weeping cherry hangs directly above the shrine, its blossoms reflected in the current below.
_facts: Small shrine at the south end of Shirakawa Canal in Gion district
Visited by geiko and maiko praying for success in their arts
Located in Gion's ochaya (teahouse) district, Higashiyama-ku
Landmark of the Tatsumi Bridge (Tatsumi Bashi) area referenced in Gion district history
Weeping cherry tree flowers over the shrine in spring

s: Three centuries of cargo on a man-made canal
w: Dug in 1611 by merchant Suminokura Ryoi to haul timber and supplies for rebuilding the Great Buddha of Kyoto, the Takase-gawa runs 9.7 km from Nijo south to Fushimi, parallel to Kiyamachi-dori. For 300 years, flat-bottomed takasebune were towed along it by teams of rope-pullers; water transport was abolished in 1920. The canal gave Mori Ogai the setting for his 1916 story 'Takasebune' — a meditation on desire, contentment, and a man being transported to exile. One original boat dock, Ichi-no-funairi, survives.
_facts: Canal dug in 1611 by Suminokura Ryoi to transport materials for rebuilding Great Buddha at Hoko-ji
Used for general logistics from 1614, water transport abolished June 1920
9.7 km long, runs from Nijo to Fushimi
Mori Ogai set his 1916 short story 'Takasebune' (The Boat on the Takase River) on this canal
One surviving boat dock, Ichi-no-funairi, preserved along the canal

s: Japan's tallest wooden pagoda — and a flea market on the 21st
w: Founded in 796, two years after the capital moved to Heian-kyo, To-ji was one of only three Buddhist temples permitted in the new city. Emperor Saga gave it to Kukai (Kobo Daishi) in 823, and the Shingon master shaped it into a mandala in three dimensions. The five-storied pagoda, rebuilt by Tokugawa Iemitsu in 1643, stands 54.8 m — Japan's tallest wooden tower. On the 21st of each month (the date of Kukai's death in 835) the grounds fill with the Kobo-san flea market: antiques, pottery, street food, the usual Tokyo sprawl compressed into a temple courtyard.
_facts: Founded 796, one of only three Buddhist temples allowed in the Heian capital
Given to Kukai (Kobo Daishi) by Emperor Saga in 823
Five-storied pagoda built 1643 by order of Tokugawa Iemitsu, 54.8m — Japan's tallest wooden tower
Kobo-san flea market held on 21st of each month, date of Kukai's death in 835
UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1994

s: 1,001 golden Kannon and 255 years of archery
w: Taira no Kiyomori built this temple for the retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa in 1164 — the name means 'hall of thirty-three bays,' a reference to the 33 forms Kannon can take. Inside its 120 m hall stand 1,001 gilded thousand-armed Kannon, most carved in the Kamakura period by three competing schools of sculptors; 124 of the originals survived a 1249 fire. For 255 years during the Edo period, an archery contest called Toshiya was held along the western veranda — participants shooting 10,000 arrows the full 120 m length in 24 hours.
_facts: Founded 1164 by Taira no Kiyomori for cloistered Emperor Go-Shirakawa
1,001 standing Thousand-armed Kannon, most from 13th century, designated National Treasures
Main hall (hondo) 120m long, from 1266, National Treasure
Toshiya archery tournament held on the west veranda for 255 years during the Edo period
124 original Kannon saved from 1249 fire; 876 replacement statues carved over 16 years

s: Founded 1637 — sake from the oldest of houses
w: Fushimi's spring water is famous throughout Japan, and Gekkeikan has been drawing on it since Jiemon Okura founded the brewery in 1637. The company name means 'laurel wreath' and the brewery is one of the world's oldest continuously operating companies, a member of the Henokiens group of bicentennial firms. The museum in the original kura (storehouse) on the Uji-gawa canal traces four centuries of fermentation: earthenware tools, giant cedar casks, sake in all its old styles. Sample a cup of junmai at the end.
_facts: Founded 1637 by Jiemon Okura in Fushimi, Kyoto
One of the world's oldest companies, member of the Henokiens group
Company name means 'laurel wreath'
Controls approximately 25% of the American sake market via US subsidiary
Fushimi known for exceptionally soft spring water ideal for sake brewing

s: Japan's largest temple bell — and a nightingale floor
w: Headquarters of Jodo-shu Pure Land Buddhism, founded on the spot where Honen (1133-1212) lived and died. The vast compound was rebuilt by Tokugawa Iemitsu in the 1630s and the colossal sanmon gate — the largest surviving temple gate in Japan, built 1619 — sets the scale. Inside the main hall the floorboards squeal underfoot: an intentional 'nightingale floor,' metal joints rubbing to alert guards of movement. In the rafters hangs a single umbrella, placed there by an architect to bring rain and ward off fire. The 74-ton temple bell requires 17 monks to ring.
_facts: Headquarters of Jodo-shu (Pure Land Buddhism) founded by Honen (1133-1212)
Sanmon gate built 1619, largest surviving wooden gate structure in Japan
Japan's largest temple bell, commissioned 1633, weighs 74 tons, now requires 17 monks to ring
Nightingale floor in main hall — wooden boards with metal joints designed to squeak as alarm
Umbrella hidden in rafters by an architect to bring rain and ward off fire

s: Guardian of the south, where wisteria sweeps the pond
w: When Emperor Kammu established Heian-kyo in 794, Jonan-gu was set to guard the capital's southern approach — the 'jo' meaning castle, 'nan' meaning south. Travellers have prayed here before journeys ever since, and the shrine's modern reputation for blessing safe movement extends to car safety. Landscape architect Kinsaku Nakane (1917-1995) made his career-defining garden here: five separate sections move through the seasons in moss, stone and water, culminating in a cascade of late-March wisteria that drags its branches into the pond.
_facts: Founded as guardian shrine of the southern approach to the Heian capital, 794 CE
Name: 'jo' (castle) + 'nan' (south) + 'gu' (grand shrine)
Shrine for praying before journeys and for traffic/travel safety
Garden designed by renowned landscape architect Kinsaku Nakane (1917-1995) as his first project
Famous for cascading wisteria in late March and early April

s: The moss temple that demands you write first
w: Koke-dera — the moss temple — requires a written application weeks in advance; you arrive, copy a sutra, then walk. Over 120 species of moss carpet the two-tiered garden in every shade from lime to near-black, a green so dense it looks applied rather than grown. The garden was created around 1339 by Muso Soseki, who restored a long-derelict temple said to have been founded by Gyoki in 731 CE on a retreat used by Prince Shotoku. UNESCO listed it in 1994 as part of the Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto.
_facts: Officially Saihoji, nicknamed 'Koke-dera' (moss temple)
Over 120 species of moss cover the two-tiered garden
Garden created c.1339 by Muso Soseki, restoring a temple said founded 731 CE by Gyoki
Requires advance written reservation, visitors copy a sutra before entering
UNESCO World Heritage Site 1994, part of Historic Monuments of Ancient Kyoto

s: Where Ryoma Sakamoto survived the night — just
w: On 9 March 1866, shogunate agents raided this riverside inn in Fushimi to arrest Sakamoto Ryoma, the revolutionary samurai who was secretly brokering an alliance between the rival Choshu and Satsuma domains. His future wife Narasaki Ryo, hearing boots on the stairs, ran naked from the bath to warn him. Ryoma fought off the attackers with a Smith and Wesson revolver, was wounded, and escaped via a storehouse to the canal. He was assassinated 21 months later. The original inn burned in 1868 during the Battle of Toba-Fushimi; today's building is a reconstruction.
_facts: Teradaya incident: 9 March 1866, shogunate agents raided inn to arrest Sakamoto Ryoma
Narasaki Ryo (Oryo), his future wife, ran from bath naked to warn him
Ryoma defended himself with a Smith & Wesson revolver before escaping
First Teradaya incident May 1862: first armed rebellion against the shogunate
Original inn burned in the Battle of Toba-Fushimi 1868; current building is a reconstruction

s: The boat railway that lit Kyoto's first trams
w: When the Lake Biwa Canal opened in 1890, Meiji engineers faced a 36-metre height difference between its upper and lower channels. Solution: an inclined railway 582 m long to haul laden barges on wheeled cradles. The hydroelectric plant at the canal's base generated enough power to run Japan's first-ever electric tram line from 1895. The incline closed in 1948; the old rails remain, embedded in a cherry-tree-lined path. Each April the trees bloom over the tracks in one of Kyoto's least-crowded viewing spots.
_facts: Built as part of the Lake Biwa Canal project completed 1890
582m inclined railway to raise boats 36m between upper and lower canal levels
Hydroelectric plant at Keage powered Japan's first electric tram line from 1895
Closed 1948; rails preserved in situ, now a park with cherry trees
Canal designated nationally as a Historic Site in 1996
